steps.title: Identify the key qualifier in Ert and Erev's experiment
steps.content: Ert and Erev deliberately varied the magnitude of stakes (10 shekels vs. 100 shekels) and found that loss aversion occurred only in the high-stakes condition. This qualification directly addresses whether the phenomenon Kahneman and Tversky described depends on outcome magnitude.

steps.title: Match the response to the correct choice
steps.content:
Ert and Erev agree that loss aversion exists but argue it is contingent on outcome magnitude — specifically, appearing only in high-stakes contexts.

Choice D expresses this response precisely: the attitude toward losses that K&T observed depends on the magnitude of outcomes.

The other choices either misidentify the focus (A addresses methodology), provide partial agreement without the magnitude qualifier (B), or wrongly suggest Ert and Erev dispute K&T's core claim (C).

That matches choice D — D is correct.
